title: Hand-lettered zine spreads
subtitle: Designing and laying out zine page spreads using hand lettering
excerpt: A hand-lettered zine has a visual character that photocopied or digital text cannot replicate: the slight variation in each letter, the tension between careful construction and spontaneous mark-making. This tutorial covers text hierarchy in a small format, how to fit a hand-lettered spread to the zine's tiny page size, and how to combine headline lettering with body text efficiently.
sourceNotes: Broad self-publishing technique, synthesised from Teal Triggs, Fanzines (2010, Thames and Hudson), lettering appendix, and standard hand-lettering practice as documented in Showit Studios, The Hand Lettering Workbook (2016).

content.text: A zine page is small, half an A5 sheet, roughly 74 × 105 mm, which means crowding happens immediately if the

is not clear. The three elements, headline, standfirst, and body, must have distinct sizes. In a small format, this means: headline at 8-10 mm x-height, standfirst at 4 mm x-height, body text at 2 mm x-height.

Fold a scrap sheet into a zine. On each page, draw a rectangle showing where the text block will sit, leaving at least 8 mm margins on all sides. Block in the headline with a solid rectangle; block the standfirst and body as line areas. This
shows whether the content fits before you commit any lettering to the final paper.

Choose one calligraphic style for the headlines throughout the zine: consistency makes the whole thing feel coherent. A simple Foundational hand at 8 mm x-height reads clearly on a small page. Keep headlines short, two to five words at most. Work on the full-size sheet before folding; a folded sheet is hard to letter on because the fold creates an uneven surface.

Body text at 2 mm x-height requires either a very fine nib or a small fineliner pen. A Mitchell Roundhand size 6 (finest) gives a 2 mm body at a standard 5-nib-width x-height. Rule guide lines lightly in pencil, write the text, then erase the guides once the ink is dry. Keep line spacing to 1.5× the x-height minimum, smaller line spacing makes small body text illegible.

The most common hand-lettered zine mistake is filling every page with text. Leave at least one spread as predominantly white space with only a single short headline. This rest page gives the eye a break and makes the text-heavy pages more readable. A small line illustration (3-4 cm square), cut and pasted from a separate sheet, gives a rough-paste visual that suits the hand-made aesthetic.
